بد گمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَد گُمان, from بد (bad) + گمان (gumān).

بدتمیز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَدتَمِیز (bad-tamīz). By surface analysis, بَد (bad, “bad; un-”) + تَمِیز (tamīz, “discernment”).

بددعا.e: From بد (“mal-, evil, inauspicious”) + دعا (“prayer, wish”).

بدر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بدر (ba-dar), from به (ba) + در (dar).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بدر (badr), ultimately from Arabic بَدْر (badr).

senses.g: an Indian jujube (Ziziphus mauritiana) or its fruit
a kernel of the fruit of the cotton plant

بدر.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit बदर (badara), Doublet of بیر (ber).

بدصورت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بدصورت (badsūrat), or from Urdu بد (“bad”) + Urdu صورت (“appearance”).

senses.g: bid'a (heresy, innovation, ie. not conforming to a religion's ideals)

بدقسمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَدْقِسْمَت (badqismat), from بد (bad) + قسمت (qismat).

senses.g: to transfer (to another school, post, etc.)
to have or get changed, or transferred (to another school, post, etc.)

ex.u: اُس نے مُجْھ سے یہ سات دَفْعَہ بَدَلْوائی۔
ex.r: us ne mujh se ye sāt daf'a badalvāī.
ex.e: She got it changed by me seven times.

بدلوانا.e: Causative form of بَدَلْنا (badalnā) with stem augmented by وا (vā)

بدلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بدل (badal), from Arabic بَدَل (badal).

ex.e: This person is such a rascal

بدمعاش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَدْمَعَاش (badma'āš), from بَد (bad) + مَعَاش (ma'āš).

بدن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَدَن (badan), borrowed from Arabic بَدَن (badan).

senses.g: by the grace (of), by the generosity (of)

بدولت.e: Compound of ب (ba) + دولت (daulat), both borrowed from Classical Persian.

بدی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَدِی (badī). By surface analysis, ِبَد (ibad) + ی (ī).

senses.g: the dark half of a lunar month (from full to new moon)

بدیع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَدِیع (badī'), itself from Arabic بَدِيع (badīʕ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بَدِیع (badī'), itself from Arabic بَدِيع (badīʕ).

senses.g: Badin (a city in Badin district, Sindh, Pakistan)

بدھ.e: Shortened from بدھوار (budhvār), from Sanskrit बुधवार (budhavāra).
From Sanskrit बुद्ध (buddha). Doublet of بت (but).

senses.g: happy (used in greetings, e.g. happy anniversary, happy birthday)

بدھو.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀩𑀼𑀤𑁆𑀥 (*buddha). Unrelated to بُدھ (budh, “Buddha; wisdom”).

بدھیا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀤𑁆𑀥𑀺𑀅 (vaddhia), from Sanskrit वध्रि (vádhri, “castrated”).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀤𑁆𑀥𑀺𑀅 (vaddhia), from Sanskrit वध्रि (vádhri, “castrated”).

senses.g: bad (including all the broader meanings associated, i.e. sinner, oppressor, dirty, disgraced etc.)
baraat (marriage procession from the groom's home to the bride's)

برات.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi برات (brat /⁠barāt⁠/) (c. 1693), from Sanskrit वरयात्रा (varayātrā), a compound of वर (vará, “suitor, bridegroom, husband”) + यात्रा (yā́trā, “journey”), literally “the groom's journey [to the bride]”.

senses.g: Brazil (a large Portuguese-speaking country in South America)

براعظم.e: Univerbation of بَرِّ (barr-i, “land (as opposed to sea)”) + اَعْظَم (a'zam, “greatest”).

برباد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian برباد (bar-bād, literally “to the wind”). Compare Punjabi بَرْبَاد (barbād), Bengali বরবাদ (borbad).

بربادی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَرْبَادی (barbādī). By surface analysis, بَرْبَاد (barbād) + ـی (-ī).

برتاؤ.e: From بَرْتانا (bartānā, “to treat; to use”) + ـاؤ.

senses.g: crockery, dinnerware, tableware; any vessel used for eating

ex.u: عورت نے برتن صاف کرنے شروع کیے۔
ex.r: ’aurat ne bartan sāf karne śurū kiye.
ex.e: The lady began cleaning the dishes.

برتن.e: From برتنا (baratnā, “to use, make use of”) + ان (an). Compare Punjabi ਵਰਤਣ (vartaṇ) / وَرْتَݨ (vartaṇ).

برج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بُرْج (burj), borrowed from Arabic بُرْج (burj), borrowed from Classical Syriac ܒܘܪܓܐ, borrowed from Latin burgus or Ancient Greek πύργος (púrgos).

برچھا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *व्रश्चक (*vraścaka, “one who cuts”), from व्रश्च् (vraśc, “to cut”, root) + -अक (-aka, “-er”).

برس.e: Inherited from Old Hindi बरस (barasa), from Prakrit 𑀯𑀭𑀺𑀲 (varisa), semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit वर्ष (varṣá).

برسات.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀸𑀲𑀸𑀭𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺 (vāsāratti), from Sanskrit वर्षारात्रि (varṣārātri, “the rainy season”). Cognate with Gujarati વરસાદ (varsād).

برسنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀭𑀺𑀲𑀇 (varisaï), from Sanskrit वर्षति (varṣati).

senses.g: a memorial held at one's death anniversary
Indian subcontinent (a subcontinent in South Asia)

برصغیر.e: Derived from Classical Persian برِ صغیر (barr-e-saġîr), an ezafe compound formed out of بر (barr, “land, continent”) and صغیر (saġir, “small, minor”)

برطانوی.e: Made up with Arabic بَرْطان (Britain) and Persian ی

senses.g: Britain (the United Kingdom, a kingdom and country in Northern Europe including the island of Great Britain as well as Northern Ireland on the northeastern portion of the island of Ireland)

Britain (great Britain, a large island (sometimes also including some of the surrounding smaller islands) off the north-west coast of Western Europe, made up of England, Scotland, and Wales; especially (but not exclusively) during antiquity)

برطانیہ.e: From Classical Persian بِرِطَانِیَه (biritāniya), from Arabic بِرِطَانِيَة (biriṭāniya) or بَرْطَانِيَة (barṭāniya), ultimately from Latin Britannia. Compare Pashto برطانيه (bartānya).

senses.g: Great Britain (a large island (sometimes also including some of the surrounding smaller islands) off the north-west coast of Western Europe, made up of England, Scotland, and Wales)
Great Britain (the United Kingdom, a kingdom and country in Northern Europe including the island of Great Britain as well as Northern Ireland on the northeastern portion of the island of Ireland)

برف باری.e: Compound of بَرْف (barf, “ice, snow”) + باری (bārī, “stream, flow, spate”)

برقع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بُرْقَع (burqa'), borrowed from Arabic بُرْقُع (burquʕ).

برقی ڈاک.e: From بَرْقی (barqī, “electronic”) + ڈاک (ḍāk, “mail”). Calque of English email.

برقی ڈاک پتہ.e: From Urdu بَرْقی (barqī, “electric, electronic”) + Urdu ڈاک (ḍāk, “mail”) + Urdu پَتَہ (pata, “address”).

ex.u: اَچّھے کاموں سے بَرْکَتیں مِلْتی ہَیں۔
ex.r: acche kāmõ se barkatẽ miltī ha͠i.
ex.e: You receive blessings from doing good deeds.

برکت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian برکت (barakat), from Arabic بَرَكَة (baraka).

senses.g: a female given name from Arabic

برکت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian برکت (barakat), from Arabic بَرَكَة (baraka).

برگزیدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian برگزیده (bar-guzīda), past participle of برگزیدن (bar-guzīdan).

senses.g: Berlin (the capital and largest city of Germany)
Berlin (a state of Germany, containing the capital city)

برلن.e: Borrowed from English Berlin, itself from German Berlin.

senses.g: a major city and metropolitan borough of the West Midlands, England
Bruges (the capital city of West Flanders province, Belgium)

برودت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian برودت (burūdat), from Arabic بُرُودَة (burūda).

senses.g: a genre of Bhojpuri folk music.

برہا.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit विरह (viraha), probably from वि- (vi-) + रहस् (rahas, “deserted place”).

senses.g: Brahma (creator god and first of the Trimurti)

ex.u: میں برہنہ ہو گئی اور نہائی تھی
ex.r: mẽ barahna ho gayī aur nahāyī thī
ex.e: I got nude and took a shower

ex.u: ہم برہنہ ٹانگوں کے ساتھ دریا میں چلے گئے
ex.r: ham barahne ṭāngõ ke sāth daryā me cale ga'e
ex.e: we walked into the river with bare legs

برہنہ.e: From Classical Persian بَرَهْنَه (barahna, “nude, naked; bare”).

senses.g: inflection of بُرا (burā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of بُرا (burā): — oblique masculine singular/plural

ex.u: لوگ جَب بَڑے ہو جاتے ہَیں، اُن کی ذِمّہ دارِیاں بھی بَڑی ہو جاتی ہَیں
ex.r: log jab baṛe ho jāte haĩ, un kī zimme dāriyā̃ bhī baṛī ho jātī haĩ
ex.e: When people grow up, their responsibilities also become bigger with them

بڑا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀟𑁆𑀠 (vaḍḍha), from Sanskrit वृद्ध (vṛddha). Compare to Assamese বড় (bor), Bengali বড় (boṛ), Marathi बडा (baḍā), and Punjabi وَڈّا (vaḍḍā).

بڑا دن.e: Compound of بَڑا (baṛā) + دِن (din), literally “big day or the great day”. Compare Bengali বড়দিন (boṛodin).

senses.g: Name of the ninth letter of the Urdu alphabet (ح).
inflection of بَڑا (baṛā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of بَڑا (baṛā): — oblique masculine singular/plural

بڑھاپا.e: Related to بوڑھا (būṛhā, “old man”). Compare Punjabi بُڑھاپا (buṛā̀pā) and Saraiki بُݙھیپا (buḏhepā).

ex.e: for one's hand to be stretched out

بڑھنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀟𑁆𑀠𑀤𑀺 (vaḍḍhadi), from Sanskrit वर्धते (vardhate, “to grow, increase”). Cognate to Punjabi ਵੱਧਣਾ (vaddhaṇā) / وَدھݨا (vadhṇā), Marathi वाढणे (vāḍhṇe), Assamese বঢ়া (borha).

ex.u: مَیں نے سُنَا ہَے کِہ جِس آدْمی کے سَاتھ میرِی شَادِی ہونے وَالِی ہَے وُہ کَافِی بُزُرْگ ہَیں
ex.r: ma͠i ne sunā hai ki jis ādmī ke sāth merī śādī hone vālī hai vo kāfī buzurg ha͠i
ex.e: I've heard that the man I'm getting married to is quite old.

ex.u: ہَمیں بُزُرْگوں کی حِفَاظَت کا کوئی طَرِیقَہ ڈُھونْڈھنا ہوگا
ex.r: hamẽ buzurgõ kī hifāzat kā koī tarīqa ḍhūnḍhnā hogā
ex.e: We must find some method for the protection of the elderly.

بس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀲 (vasa), from Sanskrit वश (vaśa), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *wášas.
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀺𑀲 (visa), from Sanskrit वि॒ष (viṣá). Doublet of بِش (biś) and وِش (viś).

senses.g: inflection of بسنا (basnā): — second-person singular present imperative

بس.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

بستہ.e: From Classical Persian بسته (basta, “sealed, closed, shut, package”).

بستی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi بستی (bsty) (c. 1657), semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit वसति (vasati).

senses.g: to settle down, to settle in, to prosper
to inhabit, to occupy, to reside

بسنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀲𑀇 (vasaï), from Sanskrit वस॑ति (vásati), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hwásati, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂wéseti, from *h₂wes- + *-eti.

senses.g: a festival observing the arrival of spring.

بسنت.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit वसन्त (vasantá). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi بسنت (bsnt /⁠basant⁠/). Doublet of بَہار (bahār) and وَسَنْت (vasant).

senses.g: a male given name, Basant, feminine equivalent بَسَنْتی (basantī)

بسنت.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit वसन्त (vasantá). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi بسنت (bsnt /⁠basant⁠/). Doublet of بَہار (bahār) and وَسَنْت (vasant).

بسیار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بسیار (bisyâr, “much, many, very”), from Middle Persian [script needed] (wsy(k)ʾl /⁠wasyār⁠/, “much, many”).

senses.g: a male given name, Bashir or Basheer, from Arabic
a surname, Bashir or Basheer, from Arabic

بضاعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بِضَاعَت (bizā'at), from Arabic بِضَاعَة (biḍāʕa).

senses.g: duck – alternative form of بَط (bat̤, “(aquatic bird of the family Anatidae)”)

ex.u: بطخ مشہور ہکھیر و ہے در قسم کا ہوتا ہے چھوٹا اور بڑا ، چھوٹی بطخ کو چنیا بدک کہتے ہیں

بطخ.e: Hypercorrection of Classical Persian بطک (bat̤ak, “duck”), a wanderwort related to Arabic بَطَّة (baṭṭa), بَطّ (baṭṭ) (which itself is from Classical Persian بت (bat), perhaps with the diminutive suffix ـه (-a)), Old Armenian բադ (bad) and Sanskrit वर्तक (vartaka, “quail”), among others. Doublet of بَطّ (batt) and بدک (badak). Cognate with Punjabi ਬਤਕ (batak), ਬੱਤਖ਼ (battax) / بَطَّخ (bat̤t̤ax), Gujarati બતક (batak), Sindhi बदक (badaka) / Sindhi بَدَڪَ (badaka), Marathi बदक (badak), Konkani बदक (badak), Odia ବତକ (bataka), Kashmiri بَطُخ (batux), and perhaps in the Dravidian languages, Telugu బాతు (bātu), Kannada ಬಾತುಕೋಳಿ (bātukōḷi), Malayalam വാത്ത (vātta).

بعثت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بِعْثَت (bi'sat), Arabic بِعْثَة (biʕṯa).

بعد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بعد (ba'd), from Arabic بَعْد (baʕd).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بعد (ba'd), from Arabic بَعْد (baʕd).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بعد (ba'd), from Arabic بَعْد (baʕd).

بعض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَعْض (ba'z), from Arabic بَعْض (baʕḍ).

بعل.e: Borrowed from Arabic بَعْل (baʕl), from Proto-Semitic *baʕl-.

senses.g: Baalbek (the capital city of Baalbek-Hermel Governorate, Lebanon)

بعلبک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَعْلَبَکّ (ba'labakk), from Arabic بَعْلَبَكّ (baʕlabakk).

senses.g: the bosom (in relation to something which is held or guarded close to it)
on one side, aside, close by
hypothetically speaking; entertain an unlikely situation

ex.u: اور اگر، بفرض محال، کوئی شخص یہ کہتا ہے کہ جتنی عمارتیں دنیا میں موجود ہیں انہیں کسی نے نہیں بنایا تو روئے زمین پر، مشرق سے مغرب تک کوئی بھی انسان اس بات کو تسلیم نہیں کرے گا۔
ex.e: And if, hypothetically speaking, a person says [claiming] that all the buildings that exist were not made by anyone, then on the face of the earth, from the East to the West, no human would be willing to accept this statement.

بفرض محال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَه فَرْضِ مُحَال (ba farz-i muhāl). By surface analysis, بَ (ba, “in”) + فَرْضِ (farz-i, “example; instance of”) + مُحال (muhāl, “inconceivable; impossible”), literally “in the case of the inconceivable”.

بقا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَقَا (baqā), borrowed from Arabic بَقَاء (baqāʔ).

بقایا.e: Borrowed from Arabic بَقَايَا (baqāyā), plural of بَقِيَّة (baqiyya, “remaining”).
Borrowed from Arabic بَقَايَا (baqāyā), plural of بَقِيَّة (baqiyya, “remaining”).

بقرعید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بقرعید (baqar-'īd). By surface analysis, بقر (baqar, “cow, bull, cattle”) + عید ('īd, “Eid”). Compare Bengali বকরা ঈদ (bokra īd).

بکاؤ.e: From بِکْنا (biknā, “to sell, to be sold”) + ـَاؤُ (-āū).

senses.g: a male given name of historical usage

بکرا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit बर्कर (barkara, “kid, lamb”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-).

بکرا عید.e: From بکرا (bakrā, “goat, billy goat”) + عید ('īd, “Eid”), literally “Eid of the goat”.

بکری.e: Inherited from Sanskrit बर्कर (barkara, “kid, lamb”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓- (-ika-).

senses.g: a tree of species Biancaea sappan (syn. Caesalpinia sappan), the sappanwood tree, source of a red dye
mute (not having the ability to speak)

بکم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بکم (bukm), from Arabic بُكْم (bukm), common plural of أَبْكَم (ʔabkam).

بل.e: From Sanskrit बल (bala), from Proto-Indo-European *bel- (“power, strength”).

senses.g: monster, hag, curse (agent of affliction, trouble or misfortune)

بلا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بلا (balā), from Arabic بَلَاء (balāʔ).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀩𑀺𑀟𑀸𑀮 (biḍāla), 𑀩𑀺𑀮𑀸𑀮 (bilāla), from Sanskrit बिडाल (biḍāla).

senses.g: Mughal Empire (a premodern empire in present day South Asia)

ex.u:
سارے جہاں سے اچھا ہندوستاں ہَمارا
ہم بلبلیں ہیں اس کی، یہ گلستاں ہمارا
sāre jahā̃ se acchā hindostā̃ hamārā
ex.r: ham bulbulẽ ha͠i is kī, ye gulistā̃ hamārā
ex.e:
Better than the whole world, is our Hindustan;
We are its nightingales, and it is our flower-garden.

بلبل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بلبل (bulbul), from Arabic بُلْبُل (bulbul).

بلغاریہ.e: From Arabic بُلْغَارِيَة (bulḡāriya). Compare Pashto بلغاريه (bulǧāryá).

بلغم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَلْغَم (balġam), from Arabic بَلْغَم (balḡam).

ex.u: ہَمیں کَام نَہِیں، بَلْکِہ مَزَہ کَرْنَا ہَے۔
ex.r: hamẽ kām nahī̃, balki maza karnā hai.
ex.e: We want to have fun rather than do work.

بلند.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بُلند (buland). Compare Punjabi ਬੁਲੰਦ (buland) / بُلَند (buland), Gujarati બુલંદ (buland).

senses.g: A historic monument in Agra, Uttar Pradesh, India to commemorate the Mughal conquest of Gujarat.
Bulandshahr (a city in Uttar Pradesh, India)
Used other than figuratively or idiomatically: see بُلَنْد (buland), شَہَر (śahar).

بلند شہر.e: Renaming of its original name, بَرَن (baran), to literally, “high city” due to its elevation of 195 metres on the Indo-Gangetic Plain.

senses.g: an ethnically Baloch person; a speaker of the Balochi language, or bearer of Balochi heritage

بلوچ.e: Borrowed from Baluchi بلۏچ (balòč), Chagatai and Classical Persian بَلوچ (balōč), with further etymology unclear and disputed.

senses.g: Balochistan (a region between the Middle East and South Asia) — Sistan and Baluchestan (a province of Iran)
Balochistan (a region between the Middle East and South Asia) — a mountainous region of Afghanistan

بلوچستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَلوچِسْتَان (balōčistān), equivalent to بلوچ (baloc, “Baloch people”) + ـستان (-(i)stān, “-land”).

بلوغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بُلُوغ (bulūġ), from Arabic بُلُوغ (bulūḡ).

بلوغت.e: From Arabic بُلُوغ (bulūḡ). Addition of ـت is likely from analogy with other nouns derived from Arabic, such as آخِرَت (āxirat), حِماقَت (himāqat), طَبِیعَت (tabī'at), عِبادَت ('ibādat), نَباتِیات (nabātiyāt) etc. Doublet of بُلُوغ (bulūġ).

senses.g: Buleda (a city in Kech district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

بلے.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَلی (balē), from Arabic بَلَى (balā).

بمبہ.e: Borrowed from Portuguese bomba. Compare Gujarati બંબો (bambo) and Marathi बंब (bamba).

senses.g: Bombay (former name of Mumbai, a megacity, the capital of Maharashtra, India)
versus; compared with, as opposed to.

بنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀺𑀡𑀸 (viṇā), from Sanskrit विना (vínā). Doublet of وِنَا (vinā), a tatsama. Cognate with Punjabi ਬਿਨ (bin) / بِن (bin), Mewari वना (vanā) Kachchi વિણ- (viṇa-), Marathi बिन- (bin-).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بِنا (binā), from Arabic بِنَاء (bināʔ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بَنّا, from Arabic بَنَّاء (bannāʔ).

senses.g: Benares (a city in Uttar Pradesh, India)

بنارس.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀸𑀭𑀸𑀡𑀲𑀻 (vārāṇasī), from Sanskrit वाराणसी (vārāṇasī) (which is a metathesized form). Alternatively, named after the Rājā Bānār, who had rebuilt the city.

senses.g: to make to form/ turn into

بنانا.e: Urdu verb set بَنْنا (bannā) بَنانا (banānā) بَنْوانا (banvānā) Regular causative form of بننا (bannā).

بنٹنا.e: Urdu verb set بنٹنا بانٹنا بانٹانا Regular intransitive formation from بانٹنا (bā̃ṭnā).

بنجارا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀡𑀺𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀸𑀭 (vaṇijjāra, “trader”), from Sanskrit वणिज्या (vaṇijyā́, “trade”) + कार (kāra, “doer”).

senses.g: Bunji (a city in Astore district, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)

بند.e: From English bun, adapted in Urdu slang as بند (band).

senses.g: to turn off, to switch off

بند گوبھی.e: From بند (band, “closed”) + گوبھی (gobhī, “cauliflower”). Cognate to Assamese বন্ধাকবি (bondhakobi), Bengali বাঁধাকপি (bãdhakpi).

بندر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بندر (bandar). Compare Bengali বন্দর (bondor), Dhivehi ބަނދަރު (baⁿdaru).
Contracted from Old Hindi बांदर (bāṃdara), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀸𑀡𑀭 (vāṇara), from Sanskrit वानर (vānara). The -d- is excrescent between -n- and -r-, for which compare پَنْدَرَہ (pandara).

senses.g: structure, composition (of poetry or a literary work)

بندوبست.e: From Classical Persian بَنْد (band) + و (u) + بَسْت (bast).

بندوق.e: From Classical Persian بَنْدُوق (bandūq), from Arabic بُنْدُق (bunduq, “hazelnut, pebble, bullet, gun”), from Middle Persian pndk' (pondik, “hazelnut”), from Ancient Greek Ποντικόν κάρυον (Pontikón káruon, “Pontic nut”).

senses.g: servant (used to refer to oneself humbly)

بندہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَنْدَه (banda). By surface analysis, بَنْد (band, “close; block; confined”) + ـَہ (-a). Doublet of بَانْدْھنَا (bāndhnā, “to tie”). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi بندہ (banda). * Sense 5 is a semantic loan from Punjabi بَنْدَہ /ਬੰਦਾ (bandah, “person; man”).

بندی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit बन्दिन् (bandin)، from earlier वन्दिन् (vandin, “praiser, bard”).

بندی خانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بندی خانه. By surface analysis, بَنْدی (bandī) + خانَہ (xāna).

بندیگھر.e: Compounded from بندی (bandī, “captive, prisoner”) + گھر (ghar, “house, abode”)

senses.g: inflection of بَنْدَہ (banda, “servant; slave”): — oblique/vocative singular
inflection of بَنْدَہ (banda, “servant; slave”): — direct plural
any binding substance (ie. cement, tar etc.)
any binding object that connects or joins two other objects

بنفشی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بنفشی (banafšī, “violet-colored”), from بنفش (banafš), from بنفشه (banafša, “violet flower”).

بنگ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَنْگ (bang). Doublet of بھان٘گ (bhāṅg).

senses.g: Bengal (a geographic region in the northeast of South Asia today divided between Bangladesh and India (particularly the state of West Bengal))

بنگال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَنْگَال (bangāl), borrowed from Middle Bengali বাঙ্গালা (baṅgala), from Sanskrit वङ्गाल (vaṅgāla), from वङ्ग (vaṅga).

senses.g: Bengali; of or relating to Bengal (the region now divided into the sovereign country of Bangladesh and the Indian state of West Bengal)

بنگالی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بنگالی (bangālī), from بَنْگَال (bangāl, “Bengal”) + ـِی (-ī, suffix forming adjectives).

senses.g: A native or inhabitant of the Sultanate of Bengal.
A native or inhabitant of the Bengal Subah.

بنگالی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بنگالی (bangālī), from بَنْگَال (bangāl, “Bengal”) + ـِی (-ī, suffix forming adjectives).

senses.g: Bengali (a language spoken in Bangladesh and the states of Assam, Tripura and West Bengal, India)
Bangali (a village in Ramkan district, Qeshm County, Hormozgan Province, Iran)
Bengali (a village in Nancowry tehsil, Nicobar district, Andaman and Nicobar Islands, India)

بنگالی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بنگالی (bangālī), from بَنْگَال (bangāl, “Bengal”) + ـِی (-ī, suffix forming adjectives).

senses.g: Bengalology; the study of Bengal and Bengalis
Bengali, of or relating to Bengal

بنی آدم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَنِی آدَم (banī ādam), from Arabic بني آدم.
